description: Gold-standard Proto-Indo-Iranian sound correspondences for Sanskrit→Avestan

rules.name: s-to-h shift (initial)
rules.description: Sanskrit /s/ → Avestan /h/ in word-initial position. One of the most regular and well-attested correspondences between the two branches, reflecting a Proto-Iranian lenition of *s > h in onset position.

rules.name: s-to-h shift (intervocalic)
rules.description: Sanskrit /s/ → Avestan /h/ in intervocalic position. The same Proto-Iranian lenition applies between vowels, though with slightly lower regularity than word-initial position due to occasional competing changes.

rules.name: dv-to-b cluster simplification (initial)
rules.description: Sanskrit initial cluster /dv/ → Avestan /b/. The Proto-Indo-Iranian *dw cluster was simplified in Avestan by dropping the dental stop and labializing the resulting onset, whereas Sanskrit preserved the cluster intact.

rules.name: shv-to-sp cluster shift (initial)
rules.description: Sanskrit initial cluster /śv/ → Avestan /sp/. Sanskrit depalatalized the proto-language palatal *ć to ś before /w/, while Avestan preserved the stop quality and fronted the vowel glide to /p/ in the cluster, yielding sp.

rules.name: dental stop fricativization
rules.description: Sanskrit dental /t/ → Avestan dental fricative /θ/ in certain intervocalic and pre-sonorant environments. This reflects a partial fricativization process in Avestan that did not occur uniformly across all positions; regularity is context-dependent.

rules.name: retroflex neutralization
rules.description: Sanskrit retroflex consonants /ṭ, ḍ, ṇ/ → Avestan corresponding dental or alveolar stops. Avestan did not develop or preserve the retroflex series that Sanskrit inherited from contact with Dravidian substrate languages, neutralizing all retroflex distinctions to their dental counterparts.

rules.name: v-to-v stable correspondence
rules.description: Sanskrit /v/ → Avestan /v/ with no change. The Proto-Indo-Iranian labio-dental or labio-velar approximant *w/v was preserved stably in both branches in most environments, making this one of the most regular and high-confidence correspondences.

rules.name: r-to-r stable correspondence (with semantic divergence)
rules.description: Sanskrit /r/ → Avestan /r/ with no phonological change. Despite the dramatically different surface forms of ṛta and aša (cosmic truth), the rhotic consonant itself is stable across branches. The surface divergence of this pair is primarily semantic and morphological rather than phonological, making it an important test case for distinguishing phonological from semantic drift.

rules.name: velar fricativization (ksh-to-x)
rules.description: Sanskrit velar-sibilant cluster /kṣ/ → Avestan velar fricative /x/ (often followed by /š/). Proto-Indo-Iranian *kṣ was preserved as a cluster in Sanskrit but simplified in Avestan by leniting the stop to a fricative, yielding x or xš depending on the following environment.

rules.name: ai-to-ae diphthong correspondence
rules.description: Sanskrit diphthong /ai/ → Avestan /aē/. Proto-Indo-Iranian *ai was retained as a full diphthong in Sanskrit but underwent a slight vowel quality shift in Avestan, lowering the first element from /a/ toward a more open front vowel, written ae in standard Avestan transliteration. This is a regular and well-documented vowel correspondence.
